providers.pitch: Confidential inference it bills as radically verifiable.
findings: Tagged AMD SEV-SNP. It isn’t. The hardware is Intel TDX plus NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs, verified straight off Chutes’ own quotes via the reseller that exposes them. No SEV-SNP in sight.
Chutes’ own API hands a client nothing it can check: one opaque 32-character `chutes_verification` token plus prompt and template SHA-256. No hardware quote, and every attestation path 404s.

So the strongest hardware on the board is the least verifiable through its own front door. The real TDX+NVIDIA quotes exist and attest.fyi checks them, but only because NanoGPT resells the infra and surfaces them. Chutes’ promise that “anyone can query the full attestation report” is still future tense in its own docs.

identity.detail: no reference fingerprint for claimed model

notes: Chutes' own API exposes no retrievable attestation: a chat response carries only an opaque 32-char `chutes_verification` token plus prompt/template SHA-256 — not a hardware quote, and nothing a client can independently check.

The real, verifiable TDX + NVIDIA quotes for this exact hardware DO exist (NanoGPT resells Chutes and exposes them, and attest.fyi verifies them), but Chutes does not surface them to its own callers — its 'radical verifiability' attestation endpoint is, per its docs, still aspirational.

providers.pitch: Confidential inference it sells as H100 with per-request ECDSA signatures.
findings: NanoGPT sells this as “H100 + per-request ECDSA.” The attestation it actually serves is Chutes’. The report carries `attestation_type: chutes` in plain sight, so NanoGPT is reselling Chutes’ confidential stack under its own name.

The seal checks out in full. Across a 5-node fleet, every Intel TDX quote chains to Intel’s SGX Root CA at TCB UpToDate, and every GPU certificate chains to NVIDIA’s Device Identity CA. attest.fyi verifies all five nodes, both chips.

The GPU is NVIDIA Blackwell (GB202), a generation past the advertised H100. And the quote arrives base64-encoded where dstack gateways hand over hex. That one detail is the whole reason it first read as unparseable.
Partial, not Pass: a verified seal proves the node and its code, not which weights ran, and no TEE model here is small enough to reference locally. Model identity stays unbound.

identity.detail: no reference fingerprint for claimed model

notes: Intel TDX fleet: 5/5 nodes DCAP-verified to Intel SGX Root CA
TCB status (worst of fleet): UpToDate
NVIDIA BLACKWELL GPUs (GB202): cert chain to NVIDIA Device Identity CA VERIFIED on 5/5 nodes
quote transport: base64 (dstack gateways hand over hex)

model binding: the TDX+GPU seal attests the Chutes node and its code, not which model weights answered — weights load from a registry outside the measured boundary, and no TEE model here is small enough to behaviourally reference, so model identity is not independently bound
provider self-reports attestation_type='chutes'

providers.pitch: Confidential, verifiable inference in Intel TDX + H100, with DCAP remote attestation.
findings: Most of the catalog is proxied, not confidential. 28 of NEAR's 40 models are closed (every Claude, GPT, Gemini, o3/o4). Closed weights cannot run in NEAR's TEE, so they are forwarded to Anthropic, OpenAI, or Google — the prompt leaves to a third party. The gateway still returns the same valid TDX seal for every model: it proves the gateway enclave is genuine, not where the model actually ran.

8 models are self-hosted and open (deepseek-v4-flash, kimi-k2.6, gpt-oss-120b, qwen3.5-122B, qwen3.6-27B/35B, qwen3.7-max, glm-5.1) — but all are 27B to 122B. None is small enough to reference on our own hardware, so NEAR cannot earn a behavioural Pass here. RedPill passed only because it serves the 7B qwen-2.5. That is our hardware limit, not NEAR's.
Coverage: NEAR's attestation is gateway-only (no GPU payload), versus RedPill's gateway TDX plus an NVIDIA GPU-CC payload.

identity.detail: no reference fingerprint for claimed model

notes: Intel TDX v4 quote, QE vendor Intel verified
DCAP signature chain to Intel SGX Root CA: VERIFIED
TCB status: UpToDate
report_data binds gateway key: yes
measurement binding: no model/weight measurement in the attestation event log

model binding: the attested config loads weights at runtime (a HuggingFace token / model-discovery service is inside the measured compose), so the seal binds the gateway enclave, not the model — a swapped or quantised model behind the same seal would be indistinguishable here
MRTD f06dfda6dce1cf904d4e2bab…

providers.pitch: Confidential inference via AMD SEV-SNP with a Tinfoil backend.
findings: Tagged AMD SEV-SNP + Tinfoil. Neither shows up. PPQ is a proxy: chat returns only reseller fields (`provider`, `service_tier`), no verification field, no attestation headers, and every attestation path returns a catch-all 404. There is no quote to check.

Behaviour is still checkable, and it passes. The served qwen-2.5-7b matches the canonical open weights and separates cleanly from a decoy, so model identity holds here. The seal is the part that doesn’t exist.

identity.detail: behaviour matches the claimed open weights, and is distinguishable from a decoy

notes: provider makes no attestation claim

providers.pitch: Confidential inference — gateway in Intel TDX plus GPU TEE (H100); prompts stay private and execution is attested on-chain.
findings: Attestation is not uniform across the catalog. RedPill returns a valid TDX quote for qwen-2.5-7b but none for gemma-4-31b — some models you can seal-check, others you cannot. Worth knowing before trusting GPU-TEE as a blanket label.

identity.detail: behaviour matches the claimed open weights, and is distinguishable from a decoy

notes: Intel TDX v4 quote, QE vendor Intel verified
DCAP signature chain to Intel SGX Root CA: VERIFIED
TCB status: UpToDate
report_data binds gateway key: yes
measurement binding: no model/weight measurement in the attestation event log

model binding: the attested config loads weights at runtime (a HuggingFace token / model-discovery service is inside the measured compose), so the seal binds the gateway enclave, not the model — a swapped or quantised model behind the same seal would be indistinguishable here

providers.pitch: Private inference in NVIDIA H100 confidential computing.
findings: Venice is the one provider here whose attestation is per-response: every TEE and E2EE answer carries a verification badge that opens a hardware attestation report you can validate yourself.
Unfunded for this run, so neither inference nor its response-scoped seal could be exercised. The NVIDIA-CC verifier built here would apply once credited. Awaiting credit.

identity.detail: no reference fingerprint for claimed model

notes: provider makes no attestation claim
